role_summary: Senior AI Engineer for a Series A AI-native talent intelligence platform, responsible for owning the intelligence layer including ranking, retrieval, and matching systems. The role requires a hybrid of deep technical depth in modern ML systems (embeddings, LLMs, fine-tuning) and a scrappy product-engineering attitude to ship working solutions quickly while establishing evaluation infrastructure.

hard_disqualifiers.description: Career spent entirely in pure research environments (academic labs, research-only roles) without any production deployment.
AI experience consists primarily of recent (under 12 months) projects using LangChain to call OpenAI without substantial pre-LLM-era ML production experience.
hard_disqualifiers.exception: Demonstrable substantial pre-LLM-era ML production experience

hard_disqualifiers.description: Senior engineer who hasn't written production code in the last 18 months due to moving into architecture or tech lead roles.

soft_disqualifiers.description: Career trajectory shows optimizing for titles by switching companies every 1.5 years, indicating lack of long-term commitment.
soft_disqualifiers.exception: Clear signal of intent to stay 3+ years

soft_disqualifiers.description: Profile dominated by LangChain tutorials or demo blogs without evidence of deep systems thinking. (must be inferred from career_history[].description free text; no dedicated field exists)
soft_disqualifiers.evidence_reliability_note: Evidence for this rule relies on free-text keyword/pattern matching over career_history[].description (no dedicated structured field exists for publications, talks, blog posts, or OSS contributions in this dataset). penalty_weight has been reduced accordingly to limit the influence of false positives/negatives from text matching.

soft_disqualifiers.description: Entire career spent at consulting firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture, Cognizant, Capgemini) without prior product-company experience.
Primary expertise is computer vision, speech, or robotics without significant NLP/IR exposure. (must be inferred from career_history[].description free text; no dedicated field exists)
soft_disqualifiers.evidence_reliability_note: Evidence for this rule relies on free-text keyword/pattern matching over career_history[].description (no dedicated structured field exists for publications, talks, blog posts, or OSS contributions in this dataset). penalty_weight has been reduced accordingly to limit the influence of false positives/negatives from text matching.

soft_disqualifiers.description: Work entirely on closed-source proprietary systems for 5+ years without external validation (papers, talks, open-source). (must be inferred from career_history[].description free text; no dedicated field exists)
soft_disqualifiers.exception: Has external validation via papers, talks, or open-source
soft_disqualifiers.evidence_reliability_note: Evidence for this rule relies on free-text keyword/pattern matching over career_history[].description (no dedicated structured field exists for publications, talks, blog posts, or OSS contributions in this dataset). penalty_weight has been reduced accordingly to limit the influence of false positives/negatives from text matching.

required_signals.description: Production experience with embeddings-based retrieval systems (handling drift, index refresh, quality regression).
Production experience with vector databases or hybrid search infrastructure.
Strong Python skills with focus on code quality.
Hands-on experience designing evaluation frameworks for ranking systems (NDCG, MRR, MAP, A/B testing).

bonus_signals.description: Experience with learning-to-rank models (XGBoost-based or neural).
Prior exposure to HR-tech, recruiting tech, or marketplace products.
Background in distributed systems or large-scale inference optimization.
Open-source contributions in the AI/ML space.

location_scoring.international_policy: Case-by-case basis; no work visa sponsorship provided.

ideal_candidate_profile.must_have_shipped: At least one end-to-end ranking, search, or recommendation system to real users at meaningful scale

hackathon_specific_traps.keyword_stuffing_trap: Matching based solely on the presence of AI keywords (e.g., RAG, Pinecone) in the skills section without verifying actual production experience.
hackathon_specific_traps.hidden_fit_trap: Overlooking candidates who lack specific keywords but have a career history demonstrating they built recommendation systems at product companies.
hackathon_specific_traps.staleness_trap: Ranking candidates highly based on perfect paper profiles while ignoring behavioral signals like inactivity (e.g., no login for 6 months) or low recruiter response rates.

hireability_scoring.description: Distinct from active_in_job_market: measures whether a candidate who reaches the interview/offer stage actually follows through. A candidate can be actively job-searching but unreliable at later stages, or vice versa.
hireability_scoring.offer_acceptance_rate_no_data_handling: -1 means no prior offer history and must be treated as neutral/no-signal, NOT as a penalty. Do not score -1 as if it were a 0% acceptance rate.

verification_signals.description: Weak positive signal that a candidate is a real, reachable, currently-active job seeker.

honeypot_detection.description: Per submission_spec.md Section 7: ~80 honeypot candidates exist with subtly impossible profiles, forced to relevance tier 0 in the ground truth. Submissions with honeypot rate > 10% in top 100 are disqualified at Stage 3, regardless of composite score. These two named patterns are checkable with pure rule-based logic (no LLM, CPU-only) against the candidate schema.

patterns.description: "Expert" proficiency in a skill with ~0 months of stated duration using that skill (named example: 'expert proficiency in 10 skills with 0 years used').
patterns.suggested_rule: Flag if proficiency == 'expert' AND duration_months < 12. Stronger signal if this holds for many skills simultaneously (the JD's example uses 10 skills) rather than just one or two.

patterns.description: Years of experience at a company exceeds how long that company has existed (named example: '8 years of experience at a company founded 3 years ago').
patterns.limitation: Founding years were sourced via web search during this session and may contain minor inaccuracies for less-documented companies (confidence noted per company). Coverage is for the 55 real companies observed in this dataset only -- if candidates.jsonl is regenerated with new company names, this table will need to be extended. Given the closed-vocabulary nature of company names in this dataset (only 63 total, confirmed by full-dataset scan), this is expected to be stable.

Accenture.source: public record (as Accenture; predecessor Andersen Consulting older)

Genpact AI.source: Genpact, founded as GE Capital International Services (GECIS); 'AI' label in dataset refers to the same parent company, not a separate young entity

Mad Street Den.source: Inc42/Peak XV (2013 founding, 2016 US incorporation per CB Insights -- using earlier date conservatively)

Observe.AI.source: PitchBook, Tracxn, Golden -- consistent across sources

Wysa.source: LinkedIn/MHA Screening say launched 2017; TechCrunch notes idea originated 2016 -- using launch year

Verloop.io.source: sources conflict 2015 vs 2016; using earlier/conservative date

Glance.source: Wikipedia, Crunchbase, LinkedIn -- formal founding; idea conceived 2015 per StartupTalky but not used as founding date

patterns.fictional_placeholder_handling: 8 of the 63 companies appearing in career_history are fictional placeholder names (Pied Piper, Hooli, Wayne Enterprises, Acme Corp, Initech, Dunder Mifflin, Globex Inc, Stark Industries) and make up the large majority of career_history entries. These have no real founding date and this pattern cannot apply to them -- skip honeypot checking for these company names.
patterns.suggested_rule: For each career_history entry where company is in company_founding_years: compute candidate_start_year = int(start_date[:4]). If candidate_start_year < company_founding_years[company]['year'], flag as a honeypot candidate (the candidate claims to have worked at the company before it existed). This is a strong, low-false-positive signal when it fires -- not a soft penalty, treat as equivalent in severity to the skill_proficiency_duration_mismatch pattern for honeypot-rate purposes.

disqualification_threshold.consequence: Stage 3 disqualification regardless of composite score -- treat as a hard constraint on the final ranking, not just a scoring penalty.

submission_format.description: Per submission_spec.md. This is an output-shape contract, not a JD-fit scoring rule -- Phase 2 must satisfy this exactly or the submission is auto-rejected before scoring.
row_rules: rank: integers 1-100, each appearing exactly once (rank 1 = best fit)
candidate_id: must exist in candidates.jsonl, each appearing exactly once
score: non-increasing as rank increases (score@rank1 >= score@rank2 >= ... >= score@rank100); ties allowed but ranks must still be unique
reasoning: optional but strongly recommended; 1-2 sentences; evaluated at Stage 4 on 10 sampled rows

penalized: templated reasoning that just inserts the candidate's name
reasoning that mentions skills/employers/experience not in the candidate's actual profile (hallucination)
reasoning that contradicts the rank (e.g. critical reasoning at rank 5, glowing reasoning at rank 95)
rewarded: references specific facts from the candidate's profile (years of experience, current title, named skills, signal values)
connects to specific JD requirements, not generic praise
acknowledges obvious gaps/concerns honestly where they exist
reasoning_quality_checks.implication_for_phase2: Reasoning strings must be generated per-candidate from that candidate's actual field values (template-filled from real data is fine; an LLM call per candidate is NOT fine under compute constraints -- see compute_constraints below). A Python string-template approach pulling from career_history/skills/redrob_signals is the safe approach.

compute_constraints.network: off -- no hosted LLM API calls (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Gemini, etc.) during the ranking step itself
compute_constraints.implication: This confirms the two-phase architecture: Phase 1 (this JD spec extraction, using a hosted LLM) runs ONCE, offline, before the ranking step and produces jd_spec.json. Phase 2 (scoring all 100K candidates) must be pure Python/rules applied to jd_spec.json + candidates.jsonl, with no network calls, completing within 5 minutes on a 16GB CPU-only machine. Pre-computed artifacts (this spec, any indexes) are fine to build ahead of time; only the final ranking step that produces the submission CSV is constrained.

scoring_metrics.implication: Heavy weight (80%) is on getting the TOP 10-50 right (NDCG@10 + NDCG@50), not just the full top-100 ordering. Phase 2 should optimize precision at the very top of the ranking, not just overall recall across all 100 slots.
